parsing_approach: Comment/string-aware masking pass (state-machine lexer, extended from java-structure with C# verbatim @"..." and interpolated $"..." string handling), then structural matching with brace-matched spans and brace-depth checks. Not a full grammar parser, but immune to the classic regex failures: declarations inside comments or string literals (including verbatim strings containing '{' or unescaped-looking quotes), spans cut short by nested braces, anonymous/local-function bodies attributed to the enclosing type.
extracts: namespace declaration (block- or file-scoped, first one in the file)
using directives incl. 'using static' and alias imports ('using X = Y;')
class/interface/struct/enum/record declarations incl. nested types, unresolved base-list (extends+implements combined, since C# uses one colon-separated list for both), attributes
methods and constructors with line spans, signatures, modifiers, attributes, overloads, and body_form (body / expression-bodied '=>' / abstract-or-interface)
simple auto-properties ('Type Name { get; set; }') with type, modifiers, accessors, attributes
known_limitations: Not a full C# grammar: no expression-level analysis, no symbol resolution (base-list names, attribute names, return/property types are unresolved strings).
Triple-quoted raw string literals (C# 11+) are not handled by the lexer, matching java-structure's equivalent gap for Java text blocks.

Full/expanded properties with get/set bodies (e.g. 'get { return x; }') are intentionally NOT extracted as Property nodes — only simple auto-properties whose accessor list has no nested braces. This matches the common ASP.NET DTO/ViewModel pattern; computed properties with logic are out of scope for this lexical tool.
Only the first namespace declaration in a file is used to qualify names; a file with multiple sibling namespace blocks (rare) will misattribute types declared in the second block.

Attributes are only recognised when each '[...]' occupies its own line directly above the declaration (stacked or single) — an attribute on the same line as the declaration (e.g. '[HttpGet] public ActionResult Foo()') is not captured.
Destructors ('~Foo()') and operator overloads ('public static Foo operator +(...)') are not extracted as methods.
Expression-bodied members ('=> expr;') locate their end by the first ';' after '=>', which would end the span early for an expression containing a ';' inside a nested lambda body — rare in practice.
Generic type constraint clauses ('where T : class') are stripped, not parsed; constraints themselves are not recorded.

Type-declaration matching rejects a small set of reserved-keyword names (in, is, as, out, ref, when, and the modifier keywords) to avoid false positives like 'foreach (Foo record in list)' being misread as a record type named 'in' — validated against this repository, where 'record' is a common variable/parameter name.
